message:
test(lexical-publish): kill-relaunch window coverage + cli-argv fuzz target

Two independent additions bundled here because they landed on the same
audit/fuzzing sweep for the index-publish and CLI-parse surfaces.

1. Kill-relaunch integration test for the Linux atomic publish path
   (src/indexer/mod.rs + tests/e2e_search_index.rs)

   Adds a Linux-only production hook,
   `maybe_pause_lexical_publish_for_kill_relaunch`, invoked inside
   `publish_staged_lexical_index` right after NEW has been swapped
   into the live slot and the prior-live generation has been parked
   at the canonical `.publish-in-progress.bak` sidecar, but *before*
   retention moves the sidecar into `.lexical-publish-backups/`.

   The hook is a pure no-op unless the test-only env var
   `CASS_TEST_LEXICAL_PUBLISH_KILL_RELAUNCH_SENTINEL` is set; when set
   it writes a JSON sentinel (stage marker + pid + live-index path +
   canonical-sidecar path) and then sleeps for
   `CASS_TEST_LEXICAL_PUBLISH_KILL_RELAUNCH_SLEEP_MS` (default 30s)
   so a test harness can race in with a SIGKILL during the exact
   narrow window where the publish is "half applied".

   The accompanying e2e test (in e2e_search_index.rs) exercises the
   intended recovery behavior: seed a fixture index → assert baseline
   search hits → spawn `cass index --full --force-rebuild` with the
   sentinel env vars → wait for the JSON sentinel to appear (proves
   we're in the right window) → SIGKILL the child → relaunch an
   identical rebuild → assert search still returns the same hits.
   This closes the "can a power-loss during the retention step leave
   the index in a state the next process can't recover from?"
   question against a live CLI invocation, not just a unit-test
   approximation.

   Helpers added:
     - cass_std_cmd: reproduces the e2e command env (HOME, CODEX_HOME,
       no-update-prompt, ignore-sources-config) via std::process::Command
       so the test can SIGKILL without assert_cmd's wait semantics.
     - lexical_publish_in_progress_backup_path: computes the canonical
       `.<name>.publish-in-progress.bak` path deterministically.
     - lexical_publish_backups_dir: computes the sibling
       `.lexical-publish-backups/` dir.
     - wait_for_publish_kill_relaunch_sentinel: polls the sentinel JSON
       with a bounded deadline (20s) and rich panic messages on
       timeout, so a flake stops with a useful trace.

   Non-Linux: the hook is compiled out (`cfg(target_os = "linux")`)
   because the Linux atomic-rename-on-dir semantics are what make
   this window a thing. Mirrors the earlier Linux-only rollback
   test landed in fd190dbe / 9ca41bf5.

2. Structure-aware fuzz target for parse_cli
   (fuzz/Cargo.toml + fuzz/Cargo.lock + fuzz/fuzz_targets/fuzz_cli_argv.rs)

   Adds `fuzz_cli_argv` to the fuzz workspace: a libfuzzer target
   that feeds structure-aware argv (drawn from a bounded vocabulary
   of real subcommand names and known long flags, with
   case-perturbation + dash-style-perturbation + arbitrary values
   and positionals) through the full argv-parse pipeline:
   `normalize_args` → `clap::try_parse_from` → heuristic-recovery
   fallback.

   The invariant: `parse_cli` must never panic on adversarial argv.
   Every input must return either `Ok(ParsedCli)` or
   `Err(CliError)`. A structure-aware corpus is used instead of raw
   bytes because normalize_args' flag-hoisting, alias recovery, and
   case normalization are unreachable from random bytes within any
   reasonable CPU budget.

   `cargo fuzz build` and `cargo fuzz run fuzz_cli_argv` are wired up
   via the standard `[[bin]]` entry; nothing in the main workspace
   changes.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
